revision.text:
{{Short description|Japanese historical epic}} {{italic title}} [[File:Taiheiki - 1698 - 1-5.jpg|thumb|''Taiheiki'']] The {{Nihongo|'''''Taiheiki'''''|太平記||}} (Chronicle of Great Peace) is a medieval Japanese historical epic (see ''[[gunki monogatari]]'') written in the late 14th century and covers the period from 1319 to 1367.<ref>[[Louis-Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]]. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA923&dq= "''Taiheiki''"] in ''Japan Encyclopedia'', pp. 923–924.</ref><ref name=Sato/> It deals primarily with the [[Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō]], the period of war between the [[Northern Court]] of [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]] in [[Kyoto]], and the [[Southern Court]] of [[:en:Emperor Go-Daigo|Emperor Go-Daigo]] in [[Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino]].
==Original work== The latest English translation consists of 12 chapters of the 40-chapter epic, and spans the period from Go-Daigo's accession in 1318 (when Takauji was still a minor vassal of the [[Kamakura shogunate]]'s [[Hōjō clan]]), through Takauji's betrayal of the Hōjō, and Go-Daigo's fall and expulsion by Takauji in 1333, to his return to Kyoto in 1338. Go-Daigo, unlike many of the [[Emperor of Japan|emperors]] before and after him, sought to supersede the power of the ''[[shōgun]]s'', and to actually rule in addition to reigning in name. Thus began a series of battles, both military and political, as the [[:en:Fujiwara family|Fujiwara family]], who dominated the Imperial regency following the fall of the Hōjō, sought to retain influence. These battles, political maneuvers, and other developments of the time are related in the ''Taiheiki''.
==Historical significance== These battles are historically very important as they led to the extinction of the Southern Court of the Japanese Imperial Line, which to this day is seen as legitimate. Northern Court members may be considered [[pretender]]s, but the ramifications are relics of history. One Southern Court descendant, [[Kumazawa Hiromichi]], proclaimed himself Japan's Emperor after [[World War II]], calling Emperor [[Hirohito]] a fraud, as Hirohito's entire line is descended from the Northern Court. Despite this, he was not arrested for ''[[lèse-majesté]]'', even when donning the Imperial Crest, because he had a [[koseki]] detailing his bloodline back to Go-Daigo in [[Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino]]. Kumazawa has been unsuccessful at creating any political change other than some sympathy.
==Analysis== Like most Japanese historical epics, the ''Taiheiki''{{'}}s tendencies towards drama and exaggeration are acknowledged, but the text is regarded as remaining mostly accurate. It is the primary and first-hand source on many of the warriors and battles of this period, and also documents elements of the fall of the powerful and historically important [[Hōjō clan]].<ref name=Sato>{{Cite book |last=Sato |author-link=Hiroaki Sato (translator) |first=Hiroaki |title=Legends of the Samurai |publisher=Overlook Duckworth |year=1995 |ISBN=9781590207307 |pages=157–158}}</ref>
==Cultural references== A line said by the pro-imperial [[samurai]] [[Kusunoki Masashige]] (regarding how "he wished that he would be reborn seven times to fight for the emperor" in an improved way) in the ''Taiheiki'' became well-known among Japanese nationalists and pro-imperialist in the 19th and 20th centuries and the legends about him became an ideal for them to follow.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Conlan |first=Thomas D. |title=Samurai Weapons & Fighting Techniques |publisher=Amber Books Ltd |year=2022 |isbn=978-1-83886-214-5 |editor-last=Spilling |editor-first=Michael |location=London |pages=113}}</ref>
[[NHK]]'s 1991 [[taiga drama]] ''[[Taiheiki (TV series)|Taiheiki]]'' was noted for its portrayal of [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]] as an agent of change against the decadent Hōjō, rather than a national traitor as generally viewed by Japanese historians.
==See also == * [[Japanese Historical Text Initiative]] * ''[[Shin'yō Wakashū]], Nanboku-chō period collection of waka''
==References== {{Reflist}}
==Further reading== *{{cite book | author=Trans. [[Helen Craig McCullough]] | title=Taiheiki a Chronicle of Medieval Japan | publisher=Tuttle Publishing | year=1979 }}
==External links== *[http://j-texts.com/sheet/thkm.html Complete text (in Japanese)] *[http://www.nancho.net/nancho/otheremp.html Japan's Other Emperor] * Manuscript scans at [[Waseda University Library]]: [http://archive.wul.waseda.ac.jp/kosho/ri05/ri05_12563/ 1603], [http://archive.wul.waseda.ac.jp/kosho/ri05/ri05_02754/ 1698]
{{Classical Japanese literature}} ]]

}}

revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
{{Short description|Japanese historical epic}} {{italic title}} [[File:Taiheiki - 1698 - 1-5.jpg|thumb|''Taiheiki'']] The {{Nihongo|'''''Taiheiki'''''|太平記||}} (Chronicle of Great Peace) is a medieval Japanese historical epic (see ''[[:en:Gunki monogatari|Gunki monogatari]]'') written in the late 14th century and covers the period from 1319 to 1367.<ref>[[Louis-Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]]. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA923&dq= "''Taiheiki''"] in ''Japan Encyclopedia'', pp. 923–924.</ref><ref name=Sato/> It deals primarily with the [[Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō]], the period of war between the [[Northern Court]] of [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]] in [[Kyoto]], and the [[Southern Court]] of [[:en:Emperor Go-Daigo|Emperor Go-Daigo]] in [[Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino]].
==Original work== The latest English translation consists of 12 chapters of the 40-chapter epic, and spans the period from Go-Daigo's accession in 1318 (when Takauji was still a minor vassal of the [[Kamakura shogunate]]'s [[:en:Hōjō clan|Hōjō clan]]), through Takauji's betrayal of the Hōjō, and Go-Daigo's fall and expulsion by Takauji in 1333, to his return to Kyoto in 1338. Go-Daigo, unlike many of the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|emperors]] before and after him, sought to supersede the power of the ''[[shōgun]]s'', and to actually rule in addition to reigning in name. Thus began a series of battles, both military and political, as the [[:en:Fujiwara family|Fujiwara family]], who dominated the Imperial regency following the fall of the Hōjō, sought to retain influence. These battles, political maneuvers, and other developments of the time are related in the ''Taiheiki''.
==Historical significance== These battles are historically very important as they led to the extinction of the Southern Court of the Japanese Imperial Line, which to this day is seen as legitimate. Northern Court members may be considered [[pretender]]s, but the ramifications are relics of history. One Southern Court descendant, [[Kumazawa Hiromichi]], proclaimed himself Japan's Emperor after [[World War II]], calling Emperor [[:en:Hirohito|Hirohito]] a fraud, as Hirohito's entire line is descended from the Northern Court. Despite this, he was not arrested for ''[[lèse-majesté]]'', even when donning the Imperial Crest, because he had a [[koseki]] detailing his bloodline back to Go-Daigo in [[Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino]]. Kumazawa has been unsuccessful at creating any political change other than some sympathy.
==Analysis== Like most Japanese historical epics, the ''Taiheiki''{{'}}s tendencies towards drama and exaggeration are acknowledged, but the text is regarded as remaining mostly accurate. It is the primary and first-hand source on many of the warriors and battles of this period, and also documents elements of the fall of the powerful and historically important [[:en:Hōjō clan|Hōjō clan]].<ref name=Sato>{{Cite book |last=Sato |author-link=Hiroaki Sato (translator) |first=Hiroaki |title=Legends of the Samurai |publisher=Overlook Duckworth |year=1995 |ISBN=9781590207307 |pages=157–158}}</ref>
==Cultural references== A line said by the pro-imperial [[samurai]] [[Kusunoki Masashige]] (regarding how "he wished that he would be reborn seven times to fight for the emperor" in an improved way) in the ''Taiheiki'' became well-known among Japanese nationalists and pro-imperialist in the 19th and 20th centuries and the legends about him became an ideal for them to follow.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Conlan |first=Thomas D. |title=Samurai Weapons & Fighting Techniques |publisher=Amber Books Ltd |year=2022 |isbn=978-1-83886-214-5 |editor-last=Spilling |editor-first=Michael |location=London |pages=113}}</ref>
[[NHK]]'s 1991 [[taiga drama]] ''[[Taiheiki (TV series)|Taiheiki]]'' was noted for its portrayal of [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]] as an agent of change against the decadent Hōjō, rather than a national traitor as generally viewed by Japanese historians.
==See also == * [[:en:Japanese Historical Text Initiative|Japanese Historical Text Initiative]] * ''[[Shin'yō Wakashū]], Nanboku-chō period collection of waka''
==References== {{Reflist}}
==Further reading== *{{cite book | author=Trans. [[Helen Craig McCullough]] | title=Taiheiki a Chronicle of Medieval Japan | publisher=Tuttle Publishing | year=1979 }}
==External links== *[http://j-texts.com/sheet/thkm.html Complete text (in Japanese)] *[http://www.nancho.net/nancho/otheremp.html Japan's Other Emperor] * Manuscript scans at [[Waseda University Library]]: [http://archive.wul.waseda.ac.jp/kosho/ri05/ri05_12563/ 1603], [http://archive.wul.waseda.ac.jp/kosho/ri05/ri05_02754/ 1698]
{{Classical Japanese literature}} ]]

Despite this, he was not arrested for ''[[lèse-majesté]]'', even when donning the Imperial Crest, because he had a [[:en:Koseki|Koseki]] detailing his bloodline back to Go-Daigo in [[Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino]]. Kumazawa has been unsuccessful at creating any political change other than some sympathy.
==Analysis== Like most Japanese historical epics, the ''Taiheiki''{{'}}s tendencies towards drama and exaggeration are acknowledged, but the text is regarded as remaining mostly accurate. It is the primary and first-hand source on many of the warriors and battles of this period, and also documents elements of the fall of the powerful and historically important [[:en:Hōjō clan|Hōjō clan]].<ref name=Sato>{{Cite book |last=Sato |author-link=Hiroaki Sato (translator) |first=Hiroaki |title=Legends of the Samurai |publisher=Overlook Duckworth |year=1995 |ISBN=9781590207307 |pages=157–158}}</ref>
==Cultural references== A line said by the pro-imperial [[samurai]] [[Kusunoki Masashige]] (regarding how "he wished that he would be reborn seven times to fight for the emperor" in an improved way) in the ''Taiheiki'' became well-known among Japanese nationalists and pro-imperialist in the 19th and 20th centuries and the legends about him became an ideal for them to follow.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Conlan |first=Thomas D. |title=Samurai Weapons & Fighting Techniques |publisher=Amber Books Ltd |year=2022 |isbn=978-1-83886-214-5 |editor-last=Spilling |editor-first=Michael |location=London |pages=113}}</ref>
[[NHK]]'s 1991 [[taiga drama]] ''[[Taiheiki (TV series)|Taiheiki]]'' was noted for its portrayal of [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]] as an agent of change against the decadent Hōjō, rather than a national traitor as generally viewed by Japanese historians.
==See also == * [[:en:Japanese Historical Text Initiative|Japanese Historical Text Initiative]] * ''[[Shin'yō Wakashū]], Nanboku-chō period collection of waka''

{{Short description|Japanese historical epic}} {{italic title}} [[File:Taiheiki - 1698 - 1-5.jpg|thumb|''Taiheiki'']] The {{Nihongo|'''''Taiheiki'''''|太平記||}} (Chronicle of Great Peace) is a medieval Japanese historical epic (see ''[[:en:Gunki monogatari|Gunki monogatari]]'') written in the late 14th century and covers the period from 1319 to 1367.<ref>[[Louis-Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]]. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA923&dq= "''Taiheiki''"] in ''Japan Encyclopedia'', pp. 923–924.</ref><ref name=Sato/> It deals primarily with the [[Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō]], the period of war between the [[:en:Northern Court|Northern Court]] of [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]] in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], and the [[Southern Court]] of [[:en:Emperor Go-Daigo|Emperor Go-Daigo]] in [[Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino]].
==Original work== The latest English translation consists of 12 chapters of the 40-chapter epic, and spans the period from Go-Daigo's accession in 1318 (when Takauji was still a minor vassal of the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]]'s [[:en:Hōjō clan|Hōjō clan]]), through Takauji's betrayal of the Hōjō, and Go-Daigo's fall and expulsion by Takauji in 1333, to his return to Kyoto in 1338. Go-Daigo, unlike many of the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|emperors]] before and after him, sought to supersede the power of the ''[[shōgun]]s'', and to actually rule in addition to reigning in name. Thus began a series of battles, both military and political, as the [[:en:Fujiwara family|Fujiwara family]], who dominated the Imperial regency following the fall of the Hōjō, sought to retain influence. These battles, political maneuvers, and other developments of the time are related in the ''Taiheiki''.
==Historical significance== These battles are historically very important as they led to the extinction of the Southern Court of the Japanese Imperial Line, which to this day is seen as legitimate. Northern Court members may be considered [[pretender]]s, but the ramifications are relics of history. One Southern Court descendant, [[:en:Kumazawa Hiromichi|Kumazawa Hiromichi]], proclaimed himself Japan's Emperor after [[World War II]], calling Emperor [[:en:Hirohito|Hirohito]] a fraud, as Hirohito's entire line is descended from the Northern Court. Despite this, he was not arrested for ''[[lèse-majesté]]'', even when donning the Imperial Crest, because he had a [[:en:Koseki|Koseki]] detailing his bloodline back to Go-Daigo in [[Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino]]. Kumazawa has been unsuccessful at creating any political change other than some sympathy.
==Analysis== Like most Japanese historical epics, the ''Taiheiki''{{'}}s tendencies towards drama and exaggeration are acknowledged, but the text is regarded as remaining mostly accurate. It is the primary and first-hand source on many of the warriors and battles of this period, and also documents elements of the fall of the powerful and historically important [[:en:Hōjō clan|Hōjō clan]].<ref name=Sato>{{Cite book |last=Sato |author-link=Hiroaki Sato (translator) |first=Hiroaki |title=Legends of the Samurai |publisher=Overlook Duckworth |year=1995 |ISBN=9781590207307 |pages=157–158}}</ref>

[[NHK]]'s 1991 [[taiga drama]] ''[[Taiheiki (TV series)|Taiheiki]]'' was noted for its portrayal of [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]] as an agent of change against the decadent Hōjō, rather than a national traitor as generally viewed by Japanese historians.
==See also == * [[:en:Japanese Historical Text Initiative|Japanese Historical Text Initiative]] * ''[[Shin'yō Wakashū]], Nanboku-chō period collection of waka''
==References== {{Reflist}}
==Further reading== *{{cite book | author=Trans. [[Helen Craig McCullough]] | title=Taiheiki a Chronicle of Medieval Japan | publisher=Tuttle Publishing | year=1979 }}
==External links== *[http://j-texts.com/sheet/thkm.html Complete text (in Japanese)] *[http://www.nancho.net/nancho/otheremp.html Japan's Other Emperor] * Manuscript scans at [[Waseda University Library]]: [http://archive.wul.waseda.ac.jp/kosho/ri05/ri05_12563/ 1603], [http://archive.wul.waseda.ac.jp/kosho/ri05/ri05_02754/ 1698]
{{Classical Japanese literature}} ]]

revision.text:
{{Short description|Japanese historical epic}} {{italic title}} [[File:Taiheiki - 1698 - 1-5.jpg|thumb|''Taiheiki'']] The {{Nihongo|'''''Taiheiki'''''|太平記||}} (Chronicle of Great Peace) is a medieval Japanese historical epic (see ''[[:en:Gunki monogatari|Gunki monogatari]]'') written in the late 14th century and covers the period from 1319 to 1367.<ref>[[:en:Louis Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]]. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA923&dq= "''Taiheiki''"] in ''Japan Encyclopedia'', pp. 923–924.</ref><ref name=Sato/> It deals primarily with the [[Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō]], the period of war between the [[:en:Northern Court|Northern Court]] of [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]] in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], and the [[Southern Court]] of [[:en:Emperor Go-Daigo|Emperor Go-Daigo]] in [[Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino]].
==Original work== The latest English translation consists of 12 chapters of the 40-chapter epic, and spans the period from Go-Daigo's accession in 1318 (when Takauji was still a minor vassal of the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]]'s [[:en:Hōjō clan|Hōjō clan]]), through Takauji's betrayal of the Hōjō, and Go-Daigo's fall and expulsion by Takauji in 1333, to his return to Kyoto in 1338. Go-Daigo, unlike many of the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|emperors]] before and after him, sought to supersede the power of the ''[[shōgun]]s'', and to actually rule in addition to reigning in name. Thus began a series of battles, both military and political, as the [[:en:Fujiwara family|Fujiwara family]], who dominated the Imperial regency following the fall of the Hōjō, sought to retain influence. These battles, political maneuvers, and other developments of the time are related in the ''Taiheiki''.
==Historical significance== These battles are historically very important as they led to the extinction of the Southern Court of the Japanese Imperial Line, which to this day is seen as legitimate. Northern Court members may be considered [[pretender]]s, but the ramifications are relics of history. One Southern Court descendant, [[:en:Kumazawa Hiromichi|Kumazawa Hiromichi]], proclaimed himself Japan's Emperor after [[World War II]], calling Emperor [[:en:Hirohito|Hirohito]] a fraud, as Hirohito's entire line is descended from the Northern Court. Despite this, he was not arrested for ''[[:en:Lèse-majesté|Lèse-majesté]]'', even when donning the Imperial Crest, because he had a [[:en:Koseki|Koseki]] detailing his bloodline back to Go-Daigo in [[Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino]]. Kumazawa has been unsuccessful at creating any political change other than some sympathy.
==Analysis== Like most Japanese historical epics, the ''Taiheiki''{{'}}s tendencies towards drama and exaggeration are acknowledged, but the text is regarded as remaining mostly accurate. It is the primary and first-hand source on many of the warriors and battles of this period, and also documents elements of the fall of the powerful and historically important [[:en:Hōjō clan|Hōjō clan]].<ref name=Sato>{{Cite book |last=Sato |author-link=Hiroaki Sato (translator) |first=Hiroaki |title=Legends of the Samurai |publisher=Overlook Duckworth |year=1995 |ISBN=9781590207307 |pages=157–158}}</ref>
==Cultural references== A line said by the pro-imperial [[samurai]] [[Kusunoki Masashige]] (regarding how "he wished that he would be reborn seven times to fight for the emperor" in an improved way) in the ''Taiheiki'' became well-known among Japanese nationalists and pro-imperialist in the 19th and 20th centuries and the legends about him became an ideal for them to follow.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Conlan |first=Thomas D. |title=Samurai Weapons & Fighting Techniques |publisher=Amber Books Ltd |year=2022 |isbn=978-1-83886-214-5 |editor-last=Spilling |editor-first=Michael |location=London |pages=113}}</ref>
[[:en:NHK|NHK]]'s 1991 [[taiga drama]] ''[[Taiheiki (TV series)|Taiheiki]]'' was noted for its portrayal of [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]] as an agent of change against the decadent Hōjō, rather than a national traitor as generally viewed by Japanese historians.
==See also == * [[:en:Japanese Historical Text Initiative|Japanese Historical Text Initiative]] * ''[[Shin'yō Wakashū]], Nanboku-chō period collection of waka''
==References== {{Reflist}}
==Further reading== *{{cite book | author=Trans. [[Helen Craig McCullough]] | title=Taiheiki a Chronicle of Medieval Japan | publisher=Tuttle Publishing | year=1979 }}
==External links== *[http://j-texts.com/sheet/thkm.html Complete text (in Japanese)] *[http://www.nancho.net/nancho/otheremp.html Japan's Other Emperor] * Manuscript scans at [[Waseda University Library]]: [http://archive.wul.waseda.ac.jp/kosho/ri05/ri05_12563/ 1603], [http://archive.wul.waseda.ac.jp/kosho/ri05/ri05_02754/ 1698]
{{Classical Japanese literature}} ]]

}}

revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
{{Short description|Japanese historical epic}} {{italic title}} [[File:Taiheiki - 1698 - 1-5.jpg|thumb|''Taiheiki'']] The {{Nihongo|'''''Taiheiki'''''|太平記||}} (Chronicle of Great Peace) is a medieval Japanese historical epic (see ''[[:en:Gunki monogatari|Gunki monogatari]]'') written in the late 14th century and covers the period from 1319 to 1367.<ref>[[:en:Louis Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]]. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA923&dq= "''Taiheiki''"] in ''Japan Encyclopedia'', pp. 923–924.</ref><ref name=Sato/> It deals primarily with the [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō]], the period of war between the [[:en:Northern Court|Northern Court]] of [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]] in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], and the [[Southern Court]] of [[:en:Emperor Go-Daigo|Emperor Go-Daigo]] in [[Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino]].
==Original work== The latest English translation consists of 12 chapters of the 40-chapter epic, and spans the period from Go-Daigo's accession in 1318 (when Takauji was still a minor vassal of the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]]'s [[:en:Hōjō clan|Hōjō clan]]), through Takauji's betrayal of the Hōjō, and Go-Daigo's fall and expulsion by Takauji in 1333, to his return to Kyoto in 1338. Go-Daigo, unlike many of the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|emperors]] before and after him, sought to supersede the power of the ''[[shōgun]]s'', and to actually rule in addition to reigning in name. Thus began a series of battles, both military and political, as the [[:en:Fujiwara family|Fujiwara family]], who dominated the Imperial regency following the fall of the Hōjō, sought to retain influence. These battles, political maneuvers, and other developments of the time are related in the ''Taiheiki''.
==Historical significance== These battles are historically very important as they led to the extinction of the Southern Court of the Japanese Imperial Line, which to this day is seen as legitimate. Northern Court members may be considered [[:en:Pretender|Pretender]]s, but the ramifications are relics of history. One Southern Court descendant, [[:en:Kumazawa Hiromichi|Kumazawa Hiromichi]], proclaimed himself Japan's Emperor after [[World War II]], calling Emperor [[:en:Hirohito|Hirohito]] a fraud, as Hirohito's entire line is descended from the Northern Court. Despite this, he was not arrested for ''[[:en:Lèse-majesté|Lèse-majesté]]'', even when donning the Imperial Crest, because he had a [[:en:Koseki|Koseki]] detailing his bloodline back to Go-Daigo in [[Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino]]. Kumazawa has been unsuccessful at creating any political change other than some sympathy.
==Analysis== Like most Japanese historical epics, the ''Taiheiki''{{'}}s tendencies towards drama and exaggeration are acknowledged, but the text is regarded as remaining mostly accurate. It is the primary and first-hand source on many of the warriors and battles of this period, and also documents elements of the fall of the powerful and historically important [[:en:Hōjō clan|Hōjō clan]].<ref name=Sato>{{Cite book |last=Sato |author-link=Hiroaki Sato (translator) |first=Hiroaki |title=Legends of the Samurai |publisher=Overlook Duckworth |year=1995 |ISBN=9781590207307 |pages=157–158}}</ref>
==Cultural references== A line said by the pro-imperial [[samurai]] [[Kusunoki Masashige]] (regarding how "he wished that he would be reborn seven times to fight for the emperor" in an improved way) in the ''Taiheiki'' became well-known among Japanese nationalists and pro-imperialist in the 19th and 20th centuries and the legends about him became an ideal for them to follow.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Conlan |first=Thomas D. |title=Samurai Weapons & Fighting Techniques |publisher=Amber Books Ltd |year=2022 |isbn=978-1-83886-214-5 |editor-last=Spilling |editor-first=Michael |location=London |pages=113}}</ref>
[[:en:NHK|NHK]]'s 1991 [[:en:Taiga drama|Taiga drama]] ''[[:en:Taiheiki (TV series)|Taiheiki]]'' was noted for its portrayal of [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]] as an agent of change against the decadent Hōjō, rather than a national traitor as generally viewed by Japanese historians.
==See also == * [[:en:Japanese Historical Text Initiative|Japanese Historical Text Initiative]] * ''[[Shin'yō Wakashū]], Nanboku-chō period collection of waka''
==References== {{Reflist}}
==Further reading== *{{cite book | author=Trans. [[Helen Craig McCullough]] | title=Taiheiki a Chronicle of Medieval Japan | publisher=Tuttle Publishing | year=1979 }}
==External links== *[http://j-texts.com/sheet/thkm.html Complete text (in Japanese)] *[http://www.nancho.net/nancho/otheremp.html Japan's Other Emperor] * Manuscript scans at [[:en:Waseda University Library|Waseda University Library]]: [http://archive.wul.waseda.ac.jp/kosho/ri05/ri05_12563/ 1603], [http://archive.wul.waseda.ac.jp/kosho/ri05/ri05_02754/ 1698]
{{Classical Japanese literature}} ]]

revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
{{Short description|Japanese historical epic}} {{italic title}} [[File:Taiheiki - 1698 - 1-5.jpg|thumb|''Taiheiki'']] The {{Nihongo|'''''Taiheiki'''''|太平記||}} (Chronicle of Great Peace) is a medieval Japanese historical epic (see ''[[:en:Gunki monogatari|Gunki monogatari]]'') written in the late 14th century and covers the period from 1319 to 1367.<ref>[[:en:Louis Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]]. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA923&dq= "''Taiheiki''"] in ''Japan Encyclopedia'', pp. 923–924.</ref><ref name=Sato/> It deals primarily with the [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō]], the period of war between the [[:en:Northern Court|Northern Court]] of [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]] in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], and the [[Southern Court]] of [[:en:Emperor Go-Daigo|Emperor Go-Daigo]] in [[:en:Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino]].
==Original work== The latest English translation consists of 12 chapters of the 40-chapter epic, and spans the period from Go-Daigo's accession in 1318 (when Takauji was still a minor vassal of the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]]'s [[:en:Hōjō clan|Hōjō clan]]), through Takauji's betrayal of the Hōjō, and Go-Daigo's fall and expulsion by Takauji in 1333, to his return to Kyoto in 1338. Go-Daigo, unlike many of the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|emperors]] before and after him, sought to supersede the power of the ''[[shōgun]]s'', and to actually rule in addition to reigning in name. Thus began a series of battles, both military and political, as the [[:en:Fujiwara family|Fujiwara family]], who dominated the Imperial regency following the fall of the Hōjō, sought to retain influence. These battles, political maneuvers, and other developments of the time are related in the ''Taiheiki''.
==Historical significance== These battles are historically very important as they led to the extinction of the Southern Court of the Japanese Imperial Line, which to this day is seen as legitimate. Northern Court members may be considered [[:en:Pretender|Pretender]]s, but the ramifications are relics of history. One Southern Court descendant, [[:en:Kumazawa Hiromichi|Kumazawa Hiromichi]], proclaimed himself Japan's Emperor after [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], calling Emperor [[:en:Hirohito|Hirohito]] a fraud, as Hirohito's entire line is descended from the Northern Court. Despite this, he was not arrested for ''[[:en:Lèse-majesté|Lèse-majesté]]'', even when donning the Imperial Crest, because he had a [[:en:Koseki|Koseki]] detailing his bloodline back to Go-Daigo in [[:en:Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino]]. Kumazawa has been unsuccessful at creating any political change other than some sympathy.
==Analysis== Like most Japanese historical epics, the ''Taiheiki''{{'}}s tendencies towards drama and exaggeration are acknowledged, but the text is regarded as remaining mostly accurate. It is the primary and first-hand source on many of the warriors and battles of this period, and also documents elements of the fall of the powerful and historically important [[:en:Hōjō clan|Hōjō clan]].<ref name=Sato>{{Cite book |last=Sato |author-link=Hiroaki Sato (translator) |first=Hiroaki |title=Legends of the Samurai |publisher=Overlook Duckworth |year=1995 |ISBN=9781590207307 |pages=157–158}}</ref>
==Cultural references== A line said by the pro-imperial [[:en:Samurai|Samurai]] [[Kusunoki Masashige]] (regarding how "he wished that he would be reborn seven times to fight for the emperor" in an improved way) in the ''Taiheiki'' became well-known among Japanese nationalists and pro-imperialist in the 19th and 20th centuries and the legends about him became an ideal for them to follow.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Conlan |first=Thomas D. |title=Samurai Weapons & Fighting Techniques |publisher=Amber Books Ltd |year=2022 |isbn=978-1-83886-214-5 |editor-last=Spilling |editor-first=Michael |location=London |pages=113}}</ref>
[[:en:NHK|NHK]]'s 1991 [[:en:Taiga drama|Taiga drama]] ''[[:en:Taiheiki (TV series)|Taiheiki]]'' was noted for its portrayal of [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]] as an agent of change against the decadent Hōjō, rather than a national traitor as generally viewed by Japanese historians.
==See also == * [[:en:Japanese Historical Text Initiative|Japanese Historical Text Initiative]] * ''[[:en:Shin'yō Wakashū|Shin'yō Wakashū]], Nanboku-chō period collection of waka''
==References== {{Reflist}}
==Further reading== *{{cite book | author=Trans. [[Helen Craig McCullough]] | title=Taiheiki a Chronicle of Medieval Japan | publisher=Tuttle Publishing | year=1979 }}
==External links== *[http://j-texts.com/sheet/thkm.html Complete text (in Japanese)] *[http://www.nancho.net/nancho/otheremp.html Japan's Other Emperor] * Manuscript scans at [[:en:Waseda University Library|Waseda University Library]]: [http://archive.wul.waseda.ac.jp/kosho/ri05/ri05_12563/ 1603], [http://archive.wul.waseda.ac.jp/kosho/ri05/ri05_02754/ 1698]
{{Classical Japanese literature}} ]]

}}

(2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA923&dq= "''Taiheiki''"] in ''Japan Encyclopedia'', pp. 923–924.</ref><ref name=Sato/> It deals primarily with the [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō]], the period of war between the [[:en:Northern Court|Northern Court]] of [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]] in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], and the [[Southern Court]] of [[:en:Emperor Go-Daigo|Emperor Go-Daigo]] in [[:en:Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino]].
==Original work== The latest English translation consists of 12 chapters of the 40-chapter epic, and spans the period from Go-Daigo's accession in 1318 (when Takauji was still a minor vassal of the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]]'s [[:en:Hōjō clan|Hōjō clan]]), through Takauji's betrayal of the Hōjō, and Go-Daigo's fall and expulsion by Takauji in 1333, to his return to Kyoto in 1338. Go-Daigo, unlike many of the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|emperors]] before and after him, sought to supersede the power of the ''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]s'', and to actually rule in addition to reigning in name. Thus began a series of battles, both military and political, as the [[:en:Fujiwara family|Fujiwara family]], who dominated the Imperial regency following the fall of the Hōjō, sought to retain influence. These battles, political maneuvers, and other developments of the time are related in the ''Taiheiki''.
==Historical significance== These battles are historically very important as they led to the extinction of the Southern Court of the Japanese Imperial Line, which to this day is seen as legitimate. Northern Court members may be considered [[:en:Pretender|Pretender]]s, but the ramifications are relics of history. One Southern Court descendant, [[:en:Kumazawa Hiromichi|Kumazawa Hiromichi]], proclaimed himself Japan's Emperor after [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], calling Emperor [[:en:Hirohito|Hirohito]] a fraud, as Hirohito's entire line is descended from the Northern Court. Despite this, he was not arrested for ''[[:en:Lèse-majesté|Lèse-majesté]]'', even when donning the Imperial Crest, because he had a [[:en:Koseki|Koseki]] detailing his bloodline back to Go-Daigo in [[:en:Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino]]. Kumazawa has been unsuccessful at creating any political change other than some sympathy.
==Analysis== Like most Japanese historical epics, the ''Taiheiki''{{'}}s tendencies towards drama and exaggeration are acknowledged, but the text is regarded as remaining mostly accurate. It is the primary and first-hand source on many of the warriors and battles of this period, and also documents elements of the fall of the powerful and historically important [[:en:Hōjō clan|Hōjō clan]].<ref name=Sato>{{Cite book |last=Sato |author-link=Hiroaki Sato (translator) |first=Hiroaki |title=Legends of the Samurai |publisher=Overlook Duckworth |year=1995 |ISBN=9781590207307 |pages=157–158}}</ref>
==Cultural references== A line said by the pro-imperial [[:en:Samurai|Samurai]] [[Kusunoki Masashige]] (regarding how "he wished that he would be reborn seven times to fight for the emperor" in an improved way) in the ''Taiheiki'' became well-known among Japanese nationalists and pro-imperialist in the 19th and 20th centuries and the legends about him became an ideal for them to follow.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Conlan |first=Thomas D. |title=Samurai Weapons & Fighting Techniques |publisher=Amber Books Ltd |year=2022 |isbn=978-1-83886-214-5 |editor-last=Spilling |editor-first=Michael |location=London |pages=113}}</ref>
[[:en:NHK|NHK]]'s 1991 [[:en:Taiga drama|Taiga drama]] ''[[:en:Taiheiki (TV series)|Taiheiki]]'' was noted for its portrayal of [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]] as an agent of change against the decadent Hōjō, rather than a national traitor as generally viewed by Japanese historians.
==See also == * [[:en:Japanese Historical Text Initiative|Japanese Historical Text Initiative]] * ''[[:en:Shin'yō Wakashū|Shin'yō Wakashū]], Nanboku-chō period collection of waka''
==References== {{Reflist}}
==Further reading== *{{cite book | author=Trans. [[Helen Craig McCullough]] | title=Taiheiki a Chronicle of Medieval Japan | publisher=Tuttle Publishing | year=1979 }}
==External links== *[http://j-texts.com/sheet/thkm.html Complete text (in Japanese)] *[http://www.nancho.net/nancho/otheremp.html Japan's Other Emperor] * Manuscript scans at [[:en:Waseda University Library|Waseda University Library]]: [http://archive.wul.waseda.ac.jp/kosho/ri05/ri05_12563/ 1603], [http://archive.wul.waseda.ac.jp/kosho/ri05/ri05_02754/ 1698]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:تايهيكي]][[de:Taiheiki]][[en:Taiheiki]][[es:Taiheiki]][[fa:تایهیکی]][[fr:Taiheiki]][[id:Taiheiki]][[it:Taiheiki]][[ja:太平記]][[ko:태평기]][[ru:Тайхэйки]][[sr:Таихеики]][[uk:Тайхейкі]][[vi:Taiheiki]][[zh:太平記]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1152189]] Wikidata name: Taiheiki [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:41, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Japanese historical epic}} {{italic title}} [[File:Taiheiki - 1698 - 1-5.jpg|thumb|''Taiheiki'']] The {{Nihongo|'''''Taiheiki'''''|太平記||}} (Chronicle of Great Peace) is a medieval Japanese historical epic (see ''[[:en:Gunki monogatari|Gunki monogatari]]'') written in the late 14th century and covers the period from 1319 to 1367.<ref>[[:en:Louis Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]]. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA923&dq= "''Taiheiki''"] in ''Japan Encyclopedia'', pp. 923–924.</ref><ref name=Sato/> It deals primarily with the [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō]], the period of war between the [[:en:Northern Court|Northern Court]] of [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]] in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], and the [[Southern Court]] of [[:en:Emperor Go-Daigo|Emperor Go-Daigo]] in [[:en:Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino]].
==Original work== The latest English translation consists of 12 chapters of the 40-chapter epic, and spans the period from Go-Daigo's accession in 1318 (when Takauji was still a minor vassal of the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]]'s [[:en:Hōjō clan|Hōjō clan]]), through Takauji's betrayal of the Hōjō, and Go-Daigo's fall and expulsion by Takauji in 1333, to his return to Kyoto in 1338. Go-Daigo, unlike many of the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|emperors]] before and after him, sought to supersede the power of the ''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]s'', and to actually rule in addition to reigning in name. Thus began a series of battles, both military and political, as the [[:en:Fujiwara family|Fujiwara family]], who dominated the Imperial regency following the fall of the Hōjō, sought to retain influence. These battles, political maneuvers, and other developments of the time are related in the ''Taiheiki''.
==Historical significance== These battles are historically very important as they led to the extinction of the Southern Court of the Japanese Imperial Line, which to this day is seen as legitimate. Northern Court members may be considered [[:en:Pretender|Pretender]]s, but the ramifications are relics of history. One Southern Court descendant, [[:en:Kumazawa Hiromichi|Kumazawa Hiromichi]], proclaimed himself Japan's Emperor after [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], calling Emperor [[:en:Hirohito|Hirohito]] a fraud, as Hirohito's entire line is descended from the Northern Court. Despite this, he was not arrested for ''[[:en:Lèse-majesté|Lèse-majesté]]'', even when donning the Imperial Crest, because he had a [[:en:Koseki|Koseki]] detailing his bloodline back to Go-Daigo in [[:en:Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino]]. Kumazawa has been unsuccessful at creating any political change other than some sympathy.
==Analysis== Like most Japanese historical epics, the ''Taiheiki''{{'}}s tendencies towards drama and exaggeration are acknowledged, but the text is regarded as remaining mostly accurate. It is the primary and first-hand source on many of the warriors and battles of this period, and also documents elements of the fall of the powerful and historically important [[:en:Hōjō clan|Hōjō clan]].<ref name=Sato>{{Cite book |last=Sato |author-link=Hiroaki Sato (translator) |first=Hiroaki |title=Legends of the Samurai |publisher=Overlook Duckworth |year=1995 |ISBN=9781590207307 |pages=157–158}}</ref>
==Cultural references== A line said by the pro-imperial [[:en:Samurai|Samurai]] [[Kusunoki Masashige]] (regarding how "he wished that he would be reborn seven times to fight for the emperor" in an improved way) in the ''Taiheiki'' became well-known among Japanese nationalists and pro-imperialist in the 19th and 20th centuries and the legends about him became an ideal for them to follow.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Conlan |first=Thomas D. |title=Samurai Weapons & Fighting Techniques |publisher=Amber Books Ltd |year=2022 |isbn=978-1-83886-214-5 |editor-last=Spilling |editor-first=Michael |location=London |pages=113}}</ref>
[[:en:NHK|NHK]]'s 1991 [[:en:Taiga drama|Taiga drama]] ''[[:en:Taiheiki (TV series)|Taiheiki]]'' was noted for its portrayal of [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]] as an agent of change against the decadent Hōjō, rather than a national traitor as generally viewed by Japanese historians.
==See also == * [[:en:Japanese Historical Text Initiative|Japanese Historical Text Initiative]] * ''[[:en:Shin'yō Wakashū|Shin'yō Wakashū]], Nanboku-chō period collection of waka''
==References== {{Reflist}}
==Further reading== *{{cite book | author=Trans. [[Helen Craig McCullough]] | title=Taiheiki a Chronicle of Medieval Japan | publisher=Tuttle Publishing | year=1979 }}
==External links== *[http://j-texts.com/sheet/thkm.html Complete text (in Japanese)] *[http://www.nancho.net/nancho/otheremp.html Japan's Other Emperor] * Manuscript scans at [[:en:Waseda University Library|Waseda University Library]]: [http://archive.wul.waseda.ac.jp/kosho/ri05/ri05_12563/ 1603], [http://archive.wul.waseda.ac.jp/kosho/ri05/ri05_02754/ 1698]
{{Classical Japanese literature}} {{draft categories|
[[Category:Japanese chronicles]] [[Category:Late Middle Japanese texts]] [[Category:Monogatari]] [[Category:14th-century history books]] [[Category:Nanboku-chō period]] [[Category:Gunki monogatari]] [[Category:Muromachi-period works]]
}}

<!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[ar:تايهيكي]] [[de:Taiheiki]] [[es:Taiheiki]] [[fa:تایهیکی]] [[fr:Taiheiki]] [[id:Taiheiki]] [[it:Taiheiki]] [[ja:太平記]] [[ko:태평기]] [[ru:Тайхэйки]] [[sr:Таихеики]] [[uk:Тайхейкі]] [[vi:Taiheiki]] [[zh:太平記]] {{wikidata link|Q1152189}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:تايهيكي]][[de:Taiheiki]][[en:Taiheiki]][[es:Taiheiki]][[fa:تایهیکی]][[fr:Taiheiki]][[id:Taiheiki]][[it:Taiheiki]][[ja:太平記]][[ko:태평기]][[ru:Тайхэйки]][[sr:Таихеики]][[uk:Тайхейкі]][[vi:Taiheiki]][[zh:太平記]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1152189]] Wikidata name: Taiheiki [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:41, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Japanese historical epic}} {{italic title}} [[File:Taiheiki - 1698 - 1-5.jpg|thumb|''Taiheiki'']] The {{Nihongo|'''''Taiheiki'''''|太平記||}} (Chronicle of Great Peace) is a medieval Japanese historical epic (see ''[[:en:Gunki monogatari|Gunki monogatari]]'') written in the late 14th century and covers the period from 1319 to 1367.<ref>[[:en:Louis Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]]. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA923&dq= "''Taiheiki''"] in ''Japan Encyclopedia'', pp. 923–924.</ref><ref name=Sato/> It deals primarily with the [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō]], the period of war between the [[:en:Northern Court|Northern Court]] of [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]] in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], and the [[Southern Court]] of [[:en:Emperor Go-Daigo|Emperor Go-Daigo]] in [[:en:Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino]].
==Original work== The latest English translation consists of 12 chapters of the 40-chapter epic, and spans the period from Go-Daigo's accession in 1318 (when Takauji was still a minor vassal of the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]]'s [[:en:Hōjō clan|Hōjō clan]]), through Takauji's betrayal of the Hōjō, and Go-Daigo's fall and expulsion by Takauji in 1333, to his return to Kyoto in 1338. Go-Daigo, unlike many of the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|emperors]] before and after him, sought to supersede the power of the ''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]s'', and to actually rule in addition to reigning in name. Thus began a series of battles, both military and political, as the [[:en:Fujiwara family|Fujiwara family]], who dominated the Imperial regency following the fall of the Hōjō, sought to retain influence. These battles, political maneuvers, and other developments of the time are related in the ''Taiheiki''.
==Historical significance== These battles are historically very important as they led to the extinction of the Southern Court of the Japanese Imperial Line, which to this day is seen as legitimate. Northern Court members may be considered [[:en:Pretender|Pretender]]s, but the ramifications are relics of history. One Southern Court descendant, [[:en:Kumazawa Hiromichi|Kumazawa Hiromichi]], proclaimed himself Japan's Emperor after [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], calling Emperor [[:en:Hirohito|Hirohito]] a fraud, as Hirohito's entire line is descended from the Northern Court. Despite this, he was not arrested for ''[[:en:Lèse-majesté|Lèse-majesté]]'', even when donning the Imperial Crest, because he had a [[:en:Koseki|Koseki]] detailing his bloodline back to Go-Daigo in [[:en:Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino]]. Kumazawa has been unsuccessful at creating any political change other than some sympathy.
==Analysis== Like most Japanese historical epics, the ''Taiheiki''{{'}}s tendencies towards drama and exaggeration are acknowledged, but the text is regarded as remaining mostly accurate. It is the primary and first-hand source on many of the warriors and battles of this period, and also documents elements of the fall of the powerful and historically important [[:en:Hōjō clan|Hōjō clan]].<ref name=Sato>{{Cite book |last=Sato |author-link=Hiroaki Sato (translator) |first=Hiroaki |title=Legends of the Samurai |publisher=Overlook Duckworth |year=1995 |ISBN=9781590207307 |pages=157–158}}</ref>
==Cultural references== A line said by the pro-imperial [[:en:Samurai|Samurai]] [[Kusunoki Masashige]] (regarding how "he wished that he would be reborn seven times to fight for the emperor" in an improved way) in the ''Taiheiki'' became well-known among Japanese nationalists and pro-imperialist in the 19th and 20th centuries and the legends about him became an ideal for them to follow.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Conlan |first=Thomas D. |title=Samurai Weapons & Fighting Techniques |publisher=Amber Books Ltd |year=2022 |isbn=978-1-83886-214-5 |editor-last=Spilling |editor-first=Michael |location=London |pages=113}}</ref>
[[:en:NHK|NHK]]'s 1991 [[:en:Taiga drama|Taiga drama]] ''[[:en:Taiheiki (TV series)|Taiheiki]]'' was noted for its portrayal of [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]] as an agent of change against the decadent Hōjō, rather than a national traitor as generally viewed by Japanese historians.
==See also == * [[:en:Japanese Historical Text Initiative|Japanese Historical Text Initiative]] * ''[[:en:Shin'yō Wakashū|Shin'yō Wakashū]], Nanboku-chō period collection of waka''
==References== {{Reflist}}
==Further reading== *{{cite book | author=Trans. [[Helen Craig McCullough]] | title=Taiheiki a Chronicle of Medieval Japan | publisher=Tuttle Publishing | year=1979 }}
==External links== *[http://j-texts.com/sheet/thkm.html Complete text (in Japanese)] *[http://www.nancho.net/nancho/otheremp.html Japan's Other Emperor] * Manuscript scans at [[:en:Waseda University Library|Waseda University Library]]: [http://archive.wul.waseda.ac.jp/kosho/ri05/ri05_12563/ 1603], [http://archive.wul.waseda.ac.jp/kosho/ri05/ri05_02754/ 1698]

[https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA923&dq= "''Taiheiki''"] in ''Japan Encyclopedia'', pp. 923–924.</ref><ref name=Sato/> It deals primarily with the [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō]], the period of war between the [[:en:Northern Court|Northern Court]] of [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]] in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], and the [[Southern Court]] of [[:en:Emperor Go-Daigo|Emperor Go-Daigo]] in [[:en:Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino]].
==Original work== The latest English translation consists of 12 chapters of the 40-chapter epic, and spans the period from Go-Daigo's accession in 1318 (when Takauji was still a minor vassal of the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]]'s [[:en:Hōjō clan|Hōjō clan]]), through Takauji's betrayal of the Hōjō, and Go-Daigo's fall and expulsion by Takauji in 1333, to his return to Kyoto in 1338. Go-Daigo, unlike many of the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|emperors]] before and after him, sought to supersede the power of the ''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]s'', and to actually rule in addition to reigning in name. Thus began a series of battles, both military and political, as the [[:en:Fujiwara family|Fujiwara family]], who dominated the Imperial regency following the fall of the Hōjō, sought to retain influence. These battles, political maneuvers, and other developments of the time are related in the ''Taiheiki''.
==Historical significance== These battles are historically very important as they led to the extinction of the Southern Court of the Japanese Imperial Line, which to this day is seen as legitimate. Northern Court members may be considered [[:en:Pretender|Pretender]]s, but the ramifications are relics of history. One Southern Court descendant, [[:en:Kumazawa Hiromichi|Kumazawa Hiromichi]], proclaimed himself Japan's Emperor after [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], calling Emperor [[:en:Hirohito|Hirohito]] a fraud, as Hirohito's entire line is descended from the Northern Court. Despite this, he was not arrested for ''[[:en:Lèse-majesté|Lèse-majesté]]'', even when donning the Imperial Crest, because he had a [[:en:Koseki|Koseki]] detailing his bloodline back to Go-Daigo in [[:en:Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino]]. Kumazawa has been unsuccessful at creating any political change other than some sympathy.

It is the primary and first-hand source on many of the warriors and battles of this period, and also documents elements of the fall of the powerful and historically important [[:en:Hōjō clan|Hōjō clan]].<ref name=Sato>{{Cite book |last=Sato |author-link=Hiroaki Sato (translator) |first=Hiroaki |title=Legends of the Samurai |publisher=Overlook Duckworth |year=1995 |ISBN=9781590207307 |pages=157–158}}</ref>

It is the primary and first-hand source on many of the warriors and battles of this period, and also documents elements of the fall of the powerful and historically important [[:en:Hōjō clan|Hōjō clan]].<ref name=Sato>{{Cite book |last=Sato |author-link=Hiroaki Sato (translator) |first=Hiroaki |title=Legends of the Samurai |publisher=Overlook Duckworth |year=1995 |ISBN=9781590207307 |pages=157–158}}</ref>
==Cultural references== A line said by the pro-imperial [[:en:Samurai|Samurai]] [[Kusunoki Masashige]] (regarding how "he wished that he would be reborn seven times to fight for the emperor" in an improved way) in the ''Taiheiki'' became well-known among Japanese nationalists and pro-imperialist in the 19th and 20th centuries and the legends about him became an ideal for them to follow.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Conlan |first=Thomas D. |title=Samurai Weapons & Fighting Techniques |publisher=Amber Books Ltd |year=2022 |isbn=978-1-83886-214-5 |editor-last=Spilling |editor-first=Michael |location=London |pages=113}}</ref>
[[:en:NHK|NHK]]'s 1991 [[:en:Taiga drama|Taiga drama]] ''[[:en:Taiheiki (TV series)|Taiheiki]]'' was noted for its portrayal of [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]] as an agent of change against the decadent Hōjō, rather than a national traitor as generally viewed by Japanese historians.
==See also == * [[:en:Japanese Historical Text Initiative|Japanese Historical Text Initiative]] * ''[[:en:Shin'yō Wakashū|Shin'yō Wakashū]], Nanboku-chō period collection of waka''
==References== {{Reflist}}
==Further reading== *{{cite book | author=Trans. [[Helen Craig McCullough]] | title=Taiheiki a Chronicle of Medieval Japan | publisher=Tuttle Publishing | year=1979 }}
==External links== *[http://j-texts.com/sheet/thkm.html Complete text (in Japanese)] *[http://www.nancho.net/nancho/otheremp.html Japan's Other Emperor] * Manuscript scans at [[:en:Waseda University Library|Waseda University Library]]: [http://archive.wul.waseda.ac.jp/kosho/ri05/ri05_12563/ 1603], [http://archive.wul.waseda.ac.jp/kosho/ri05/ri05_02754/ 1698]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:تايهيكي]][[de:Taiheiki]][[en:Taiheiki]][[es:Taiheiki]][[fa:تایهیکی]][[fr:Taiheiki]][[id:Taiheiki]][[it:Taiheiki]][[ja:太平記]][[ko:태평기]][[ru:Тайхэйки]][[sr:Таихеики]][[uk:Тайхейкі]][[vi:Taiheiki]][[zh:太平記]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:تايهيكي]][[de:Taiheiki]][[en:Taiheiki]][[es:Taiheiki]][[fa:تایهیکی]][[fr:Taiheiki]][[id:Taiheiki]][[it:Taiheiki]][[ja:太平記]][[ko:태평기]][[ru:Тайхэйки]][[sr:Таихеики]][[uk:Тайхейкі]][[vi:Taiheiki]][[zh:太平記]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:تايهيكي]][[de:Taiheiki]][[en:Taiheiki]][[es:Taiheiki]][[fa:تایهیکی]][[fr:Taiheiki]][[id:Taiheiki]][[it:Taiheiki]][[ja:太平記]][[ko:태평기]][[ru:Тайхэйки]][[sr:Таихеики]][[uk:Тайхейкі]][[vi:Taiheiki]][[zh:太平記]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1152189]] Wikidata name: Taiheiki [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:41, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Japanese historical epic}} {{italic title}} [[File:Taiheiki - 1698 - 1-5.jpg|thumb|''Taiheiki'']] The {{Nihongo|'''''Taiheiki'''''|太平記||}} (Chronicle of Great Peace) is a medieval Japanese historical epic (see ''[[:en:Gunki monogatari|Gunki monogatari]]'') written in the late 14th century and covers the period from 1319 to 1367.<ref>[[:en:Louis Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]]. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA923&dq= "''Taiheiki''"] in ''Japan Encyclopedia'', pp. 923–924.</ref><ref name=Sato/> It deals primarily with the [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō]], the period of war between the [[:en:Northern Court|Northern Court]] of [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]] in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], and the [[Southern Court]] of [[:en:Emperor Go-Daigo|Emperor Go-Daigo]] in [[:en:Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino]].
==Original work== The latest English translation consists of 12 chapters of the 40-chapter epic, and spans the period from Go-Daigo's accession in 1318 (when Takauji was still a minor vassal of the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]]'s [[:en:Hōjō clan|Hōjō clan]]), through Takauji's betrayal of the Hōjō, and Go-Daigo's fall and expulsion by Takauji in 1333, to his return to Kyoto in 1338. Go-Daigo, unlike many of the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|emperors]] before and after him, sought to supersede the power of the ''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]s'', and to actually rule in addition to reigning in name. Thus began a series of battles, both military and political, as the [[:en:Fujiwara family|Fujiwara family]], who dominated the Imperial regency following the fall of the Hōjō, sought to retain influence. These battles, political maneuvers, and other developments of the time are related in the ''Taiheiki''.
==Historical significance== These battles are historically very important as they led to the extinction of the Southern Court of the Japanese Imperial Line, which to this day is seen as legitimate. Northern Court members may be considered [[:en:Pretender|Pretender]]s, but the ramifications are relics of history. One Southern Court descendant, [[:en:Kumazawa Hiromichi|Kumazawa Hiromichi]], proclaimed himself Japan's Emperor after [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], calling Emperor [[:en:Hirohito|Hirohito]] a fraud, as Hirohito's entire line is descended from the Northern Court. Despite this, he was not arrested for ''[[:en:Lèse-majesté|Lèse-majesté]]'', even when donning the Imperial Crest, because he had a [[:en:Koseki|Koseki]] detailing his bloodline back to Go-Daigo in [[:en:Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino]]. Kumazawa has been unsuccessful at creating any political change other than some sympathy.
==Analysis== Like most Japanese historical epics, the ''Taiheiki''{{'}}s tendencies towards drama and exaggeration are acknowledged, but the text is regarded as remaining mostly accurate. It is the primary and first-hand source on many of the warriors and battles of this period, and also documents elements of the fall of the powerful and historically important [[:en:Hōjō clan|Hōjō clan]].<ref name=Sato>{{Cite book |last=Sato |author-link=Hiroaki Sato (translator) |first=Hiroaki |title=Legends of the Samurai |publisher=Overlook Duckworth |year=1995 |ISBN=9781590207307 |pages=157–158}}</ref>
==Cultural references== A line said by the pro-imperial [[:en:Samurai|Samurai]] [[Kusunoki Masashige]] (regarding how "he wished that he would be reborn seven times to fight for the emperor" in an improved way) in the ''Taiheiki'' became well-known among Japanese nationalists and pro-imperialist in the 19th and 20th centuries and the legends about him became an ideal for them to follow.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Conlan |first=Thomas D. |title=Samurai Weapons & Fighting Techniques |publisher=Amber Books Ltd |year=2022 |isbn=978-1-83886-214-5 |editor-last=Spilling |editor-first=Michael |location=London |pages=113}}</ref>
[[:en:NHK|NHK]]'s 1991 [[:en:Taiga drama|Taiga drama]] ''[[:en:Taiheiki (TV series)|Taiheiki]]'' was noted for its portrayal of [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]] as an agent of change against the decadent Hōjō, rather than a national traitor as generally viewed by Japanese historians.
==See also == * [[:en:Japanese Historical Text Initiative|Japanese Historical Text Initiative]] * ''[[:en:Shin'yō Wakashū|Shin'yō Wakashū]], Nanboku-chō period collection of waka''
==References== {{Reflist}}
==Further reading== *{{cite book | author=Trans. [[Helen Craig McCullough]] | title=Taiheiki a Chronicle of Medieval Japan | publisher=Tuttle Publishing | year=1979 }}
==External links== *[http://j-texts.com/sheet/thkm.html Complete text (in Japanese)] *[http://www.nancho.net/nancho/otheremp.html Japan's Other Emperor] * Manuscript scans at [[:en:Waseda University Library|Waseda University Library]]: [http://archive.wul.waseda.ac.jp/kosho/ri05/ri05_12563/ 1603], [http://archive.wul.waseda.ac.jp/kosho/ri05/ri05_02754/ 1698]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:تايهيكي]][[de:Taiheiki]][[en:Taiheiki]][[es:Taiheiki]][[fa:تایهیکی]][[fr:Taiheiki]][[id:Taiheiki]][[it:Taiheiki]][[ja:太平記]][[ko:태평기]][[ru:Тайхэйки]][[sr:Таихеики]][[uk:Тайхейкі]][[vi:Taiheiki]][[zh:太平記]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:تايهيكي]][[de:Taiheiki]][[en:Taiheiki]][[es:Taiheiki]][[fa:تایهیکی]][[fr:Taiheiki]][[id:Taiheiki]][[it:Taiheiki]][[ja:太平記]][[ko:태평기]][[ru:Тайхэйки]][[sr:Таихеики]][[uk:Тайхейкі]][[vi:Taiheiki]][[zh:太平記]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:تايهيكي]][[de:Taiheiki]][[en:Taiheiki]][[es:Taiheiki]][[fa:تایهیکی]][[fr:Taiheiki]][[id:Taiheiki]][[it:Taiheiki]][[ja:太平記]][[ko:태평기]][[ru:Тайхэйки]][[sr:Таихеики]][[uk:Тайхейкі]][[vi:Taiheiki]][[zh:太平記]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1152189]] Wikidata name: Taiheiki [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:41, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Japanese historical epic}} {{italic title}} [[File:Taiheiki - 1698 - 1-5.jpg|thumb|''Taiheiki'']] The {{Nihongo|'''''Taiheiki'''''|太平記||}} (Chronicle of Great Peace) is a medieval Japanese historical epic (see ''[[:en:Gunki monogatari|Gunki monogatari]]'') written in the late 14th century and covers the period from 1319 to 1367.<ref>[[:en:Louis Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]]. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA923&dq= "''Taiheiki''"] in ''Japan Encyclopedia'', pp. 923–924.</ref><ref name=Sato/> It deals primarily with the [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō]], the period of war between the [[:en:Northern Court|Northern Court]] of [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]] in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], and the [[Southern Court]] of [[:en:Emperor Go-Daigo|Emperor Go-Daigo]] in [[:en:Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino]].
==Original work== The latest English translation consists of 12 chapters of the 40-chapter epic, and spans the period from Go-Daigo's accession in 1318 (when Takauji was still a minor vassal of the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]]'s [[:en:Hōjō clan|Hōjō clan]]), through Takauji's betrayal of the Hōjō, and Go-Daigo's fall and expulsion by Takauji in 1333, to his return to Kyoto in 1338. Go-Daigo, unlike many of the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|emperors]] before and after him, sought to supersede the power of the ''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]s'', and to actually rule in addition to reigning in name. Thus began a series of battles, both military and political, as the [[:en:Fujiwara family|Fujiwara family]], who dominated the Imperial regency following the fall of the Hōjō, sought to retain influence. These battles, political maneuvers, and other developments of the time are related in the ''Taiheiki''.
==Historical significance== These battles are historically very important as they led to the extinction of the Southern Court of the Japanese Imperial Line, which to this day is seen as legitimate. Northern Court members may be considered [[:en:Pretender|Pretender]]s, but the ramifications are relics of history. One Southern Court descendant, [[:en:Kumazawa Hiromichi|Kumazawa Hiromichi]], proclaimed himself Japan's Emperor after [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], calling Emperor [[:en:Hirohito|Hirohito]] a fraud, as Hirohito's entire line is descended from the Northern Court. Despite this, he was not arrested for ''[[:en:Lèse-majesté|Lèse-majesté]]'', even when donning the Imperial Crest, because he had a [[:en:Koseki|Koseki]] detailing his bloodline back to Go-Daigo in [[:en:Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino]]. Kumazawa has been unsuccessful at creating any political change other than some sympathy.
==Analysis== Like most Japanese historical epics, the ''Taiheiki''{{'}}s tendencies towards drama and exaggeration are acknowledged, but the text is regarded as remaining mostly accurate. It is the primary and first-hand source on many of the warriors and battles of this period, and also documents elements of the fall of the powerful and historically important [[:en:Hōjō clan|Hōjō clan]].<ref name=Sato>{{Cite book |last=Sato |author-link=Hiroaki Sato (translator) |first=Hiroaki |title=Legends of the Samurai |publisher=Overlook Duckworth |year=1995 |ISBN=9781590207307 |pages=157–158}}</ref>
==Cultural references== A line said by the pro-imperial [[:en:Samurai|Samurai]] [[Kusunoki Masashige]] (regarding how "he wished that he would be reborn seven times to fight for the emperor" in an improved way) in the ''Taiheiki'' became well-known among Japanese nationalists and pro-imperialist in the 19th and 20th centuries and the legends about him became an ideal for them to follow.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Conlan |first=Thomas D. |title=Samurai Weapons & Fighting Techniques |publisher=Amber Books Ltd |year=2022 |isbn=978-1-83886-214-5 |editor-last=Spilling |editor-first=Michael |location=London |pages=113}}</ref>
[[:en:NHK|NHK]]'s 1991 [[:en:Taiga drama|Taiga drama]] ''[[:en:Taiheiki (TV series)|Taiheiki]]'' was noted for its portrayal of [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]] as an agent of change against the decadent Hōjō, rather than a national traitor as generally viewed by Japanese historians.
==See also == * [[:en:Japanese Historical Text Initiative|Japanese Historical Text Initiative]] * ''[[:en:Shin'yō Wakashū|Shin'yō Wakashū]], Nanboku-chō period collection of waka''
==References== {{Reflist}}
==Further reading== *{{cite book | author=Trans. [[Helen Craig McCullough]] | title=Taiheiki a Chronicle of Medieval Japan | publisher=Tuttle Publishing | year=1979 }}
==External links== *[http://j-texts.com/sheet/thkm.html Complete text (in Japanese)] *[http://www.nancho.net/nancho/otheremp.html Japan's Other Emperor] * Manuscript scans at [[:en:Waseda University Library|Waseda University Library]]: [http://archive.wul.waseda.ac.jp/kosho/ri05/ri05_12563/ 1603], [http://archive.wul.waseda.ac.jp/kosho/ri05/ri05_02754/ 1698]
{{Classical Japanese literature}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:تايهيكي]][[de:Taiheiki]][[en:Taiheiki]][[es:Taiheiki]][[fa:تایهیکی]][[fr:Taiheiki]][[id:Taiheiki]][[it:Taiheiki]][[ja:太平記]][[ko:태평기]][[ru:Тайхэйки]][[sr:Таихеики]][[uk:Тайхейкі]][[vi:Taiheiki]][[zh:太平記]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:تايهيكي]][[de:Taiheiki]][[en:Taiheiki]][[es:Taiheiki]][[fa:تایهیکی]][[fr:Taiheiki]][[id:Taiheiki]][[it:Taiheiki]][[ja:太平記]][[ko:태평기]][[ru:Тайхэйки]][[sr:Таихеики]][[uk:Тайхейкі]][[vi:Taiheiki]][[zh:太平記]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:تايهيكي]][[de:Taiheiki]][[en:Taiheiki]][[es:Taiheiki]][[fa:تایهیکی]][[fr:Taiheiki]][[id:Taiheiki]][[it:Taiheiki]][[ja:太平記]][[ko:태평기]][[ru:Тайхэйки]][[sr:Таихеики]][[uk:Тайхейкі]][[vi:Taiheiki]][[zh:太平記]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1152189]] Wikidata name: Taiheiki [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:41, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Japanese historical epic}} {{italic title}} [[File:Taiheiki - 1698 - 1-5.jpg|thumb|''Taiheiki'']] The {{Nihongo|'''''Taiheiki'''''|太平記||}} (Chronicle of Great Peace) is a medieval Japanese historical epic (see ''{{ill|Gunki monogatari|en|Gunki monogatari|WD=Q587327|lt=Gunki monogatari}}'') written in the late 14th century and covers the period from 1319 to 1367.<ref>{{ill|Louis Frédéric|en|Louis Frédéric|WD=Q726040|lt=Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric}}. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA923&dq= "''Taiheiki''"] in ''Japan Encyclopedia'', pp. 923–924.</ref><ref name=Sato/> It deals primarily with the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|Nanboku-chō period|WD=Q264012|lt=Nanboku-chō}}, the period of war between the {{ill|Northern Court|en|Northern Court|WD=Q1201819|lt=Northern Court}} of {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|en|Ashikaga Takauji|WD=Q297107|lt=Ashikaga Takauji}} in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}}, and the [[Southern Court]] of {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|en|Emperor Go-Daigo|WD=Q313942|lt=Emperor Go-Daigo}} in {{ill|Yoshino, Nara|en|Yoshino, Nara|WD=Q1203731|lt=Yoshino}}.
==Original work== The latest English translation consists of 12 chapters of the 40-chapter epic, and spans the period from Go-Daigo's accession in 1318 (when Takauji was still a minor vassal of the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|en|Kamakura shogunate|WD=Q736839|lt=Kamakura shogunate}}'s {{ill|Hōjō clan|en|Hōjō clan|WD=Q834364|lt=Hōjō clan}}), through Takauji's betrayal of the Hōjō, and Go-Daigo's fall and expulsion by Takauji in 1333, to his return to Kyoto in 1338. Go-Daigo, unlike many of the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|WD=Q208233|lt=emperors}} before and after him, sought to supersede the power of the ''{{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun}}s'', and to actually rule in addition to reigning in name. Thus began a series of battles, both military and political, as the [[:en:Fujiwara family|Fujiwara family]], who dominated the Imperial regency following the fall of the Hōjō, sought to retain influence. These battles, political maneuvers, and other developments of the time are related in the ''Taiheiki''.
==Historical significance== These battles are historically very important as they led to the extinction of the Southern Court of the Japanese Imperial Line, which to this day is seen as legitimate. Northern Court members may be considered {{ill|Pretender|en|Pretender|WD=Q845929|lt=Pretender}}s, but the ramifications are relics of history. One Southern Court descendant, {{ill|Kumazawa Hiromichi|en|Kumazawa Hiromichi|WD=Q1094280|lt=Kumazawa Hiromichi}}, proclaimed himself Japan's Emperor after {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War}}, calling Emperor {{ill|Hirohito|en|Hirohito|WD=Q34479|lt=Hirohito}} a fraud, as Hirohito's entire line is descended from the Northern Court. Despite this, he was not arrested for ''{{ill|Lèse-majesté|en|Lèse-majesté|WD=Q1134057|lt=Lèse-majesté}}'', even when donning the Imperial Crest, because he had a {{ill|Koseki|en|Koseki|WD=Q2004750|lt=Koseki}} detailing his bloodline back to Go-Daigo in {{ill|Yoshino, Nara|en|Yoshino, Nara|WD=Q1203731|lt=Yoshino}}. Kumazawa has been unsuccessful at creating any political change other than some sympathy.
==Analysis== Like most Japanese historical epics, the ''Taiheiki''{{'}}s tendencies towards drama and exaggeration are acknowledged, but the text is regarded as remaining mostly accurate. It is the primary and first-hand source on many of the warriors and battles of this period, and also documents elements of the fall of the powerful and historically important {{ill|Hōjō clan|en|Hōjō clan|WD=Q834364|lt=Hōjō clan}}.<ref name=Sato>{{Cite book |last=Sato |author-link=Hiroaki Sato (translator) |first=Hiroaki |title=Legends of the Samurai |publisher=Overlook Duckworth |year=1995 |ISBN=9781590207307 |pages=157–158}}</ref>
==Cultural references== A line said by the pro-imperial {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|WD=Q38142|lt=Samurai}} [[Kusunoki Masashige]] (regarding how "he wished that he would be reborn seven times to fight for the emperor" in an improved way) in the ''Taiheiki'' became well-known among Japanese nationalists and pro-imperialist in the 19th and 20th centuries and the legends about him became an ideal for them to follow.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Conlan |first=Thomas D. |title=Samurai Weapons & Fighting Techniques |publisher=Amber Books Ltd |year=2022 |isbn=978-1-83886-214-5 |editor-last=Spilling |editor-first=Michael |location=London |pages=113}}</ref>
{{ill|NHK|en|NHK|WD=Q212128|lt=NHK}}'s 1991 {{ill|Taiga drama|en|Taiga drama|WD=Q1200935|lt=Taiga drama}} ''{{ill|Taiheiki (TV series)|en|Taiheiki (TV series)|WD=Q10941476|lt=Taiheiki}}'' was noted for its portrayal of {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|en|Ashikaga Takauji|WD=Q297107|lt=Ashikaga Takauji}} as an agent of change against the decadent Hōjō, rather than a national traitor as generally viewed by Japanese historians.
==See also == * {{ill|Japanese Historical Text Initiative|en|Japanese Historical Text Initiative|WD=Q16973361|lt=Japanese Historical Text Initiative}} * ''{{ill|Shin'yō Wakashū|en|Shin'yō Wakashū|WD=Q3006175|lt=Shin'yō Wakashū}}, Nanboku-chō period collection of waka''
==References== {{Reflist}}
==Further reading== *{{cite book | author=Trans. [[Helen Craig McCullough]] | title=Taiheiki a Chronicle of Medieval Japan | publisher=Tuttle Publishing | year=1979 }}
==External links== *[http://j-texts.com/sheet/thkm.html Complete text (in Japanese)] *[http://www.nancho.net/nancho/otheremp.html Japan's Other Emperor] * Manuscript scans at {{ill|Waseda University Library|en|Waseda University Library|WD=Q7971387|lt=Waseda University Library}}: [http://archive.wul.waseda.ac.jp/kosho/ri05/ri05_12563/ 1603], [http://archive.wul.waseda.ac.jp/kosho/ri05/ri05_02754/ 1698]
{{Classical Japanese literature}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:تايهيكي]][[de:Taiheiki]][[en:Taiheiki]][[es:Taiheiki]][[fa:تایهیکی]][[fr:Taiheiki]][[id:Taiheiki]][[it:Taiheiki]][[ja:太平記]][[ko:태평기]][[ru:Тайхэйки]][[sr:Таихеики]][[uk:Тайхейкі]][[vi:Taiheiki]][[zh:太平記]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:تايهيكي]][[de:Taiheiki]][[en:Taiheiki]][[es:Taiheiki]][[fa:تایهیکی]][[fr:Taiheiki]][[id:Taiheiki]][[it:Taiheiki]][[ja:太平記]][[ko:태평기]][[ru:Тайхэйки]][[sr:Таихеики]][[uk:Тайхейкі]][[vi:Taiheiki]][[zh:太平記]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:تايهيكي]][[de:Taiheiki]][[en:Taiheiki]][[es:Taiheiki]][[fa:تایهیکی]][[fr:Taiheiki]][[id:Taiheiki]][[it:Taiheiki]][[ja:太平記]][[ko:태평기]][[ru:Тайхэйки]][[sr:Таихеики]][[uk:Тайхейкі]][[vi:Taiheiki]][[zh:太平記]]
{{Short description|Japanese historical epic}} {{italic title}} [[File:Taiheiki - 1698 - 1-5.jpg|thumb|''Taiheiki'']] The {{Nihongo|'''''Taiheiki'''''|太平記||}} (Chronicle of Great Peace) is a medieval Japanese historical epic (see ''{{ill|Gunki monogatari|en|Gunki monogatari|WD=Q587327|lt=Gunki monogatari}}'') written in the late 14th century and covers the period from 1319 to 1367.<ref>{{ill|Louis Frédéric|en|Louis Frédéric|WD=Q726040|lt=Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric}}. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA923&dq= "''Taiheiki''"] in ''Japan Encyclopedia'', pp. 923–924.</ref><ref name=Sato/> It deals primarily with the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|Nanboku-chō period|WD=Q264012|lt=Nanboku-chō}}, the period of war between the {{ill|Northern Court|en|Northern Court|WD=Q1201819|lt=Northern Court}} of {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|en|Ashikaga Takauji|WD=Q297107|lt=Ashikaga Takauji}} in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}}, and the [[Southern Court]] of {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|en|Emperor Go-Daigo|WD=Q313942|lt=Emperor Go-Daigo}} in {{ill|Yoshino, Nara|en|Yoshino, Nara|WD=Q1203731|lt=Yoshino}}.
==Original work== The latest English translation consists of 12 chapters of the 40-chapter epic, and spans the period from Go-Daigo's accession in 1318 (when Takauji was still a minor vassal of the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|en|Kamakura shogunate|WD=Q736839|lt=Kamakura shogunate}}'s {{ill|Hōjō clan|en|Hōjō clan|WD=Q834364|lt=Hōjō clan}}), through Takauji's betrayal of the Hōjō, and Go-Daigo's fall and expulsion by Takauji in 1333, to his return to Kyoto in 1338. Go-Daigo, unlike many of the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|WD=Q208233|lt=emperors}} before and after him, sought to supersede the power of the ''{{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun}}s'', and to actually rule in addition to reigning in name. Thus began a series of battles, both military and political, as the [[:en:Fujiwara family|Fujiwara family]], who dominated the Imperial regency following the fall of the Hōjō, sought to retain influence. These battles, political maneuvers, and other developments of the time are related in the ''Taiheiki''.
==Historical significance== These battles are historically very important as they led to the extinction of the Southern Court of the Japanese Imperial Line, which to this day is seen as legitimate. Northern Court members may be considered {{ill|Pretender|en|Pretender|WD=Q845929|lt=Pretender}}s, but the ramifications are relics of history. One Southern Court descendant, {{ill|Kumazawa Hiromichi|en|Kumazawa Hiromichi|WD=Q1094280|lt=Kumazawa Hiromichi}}, proclaimed himself Japan's Emperor after {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War}}, calling Emperor {{ill|Hirohito|en|Hirohito|WD=Q34479|lt=Hirohito}} a fraud, as Hirohito's entire line is descended from the Northern Court. Despite this, he was not arrested for ''{{ill|Lèse-majesté|en|Lèse-majesté|WD=Q1134057|lt=Lèse-majesté}}'', even when donning the Imperial Crest, because he had a {{ill|Koseki|en|Koseki|WD=Q2004750|lt=Koseki}} detailing his bloodline back to Go-Daigo in {{ill|Yoshino, Nara|en|Yoshino, Nara|WD=Q1203731|lt=Yoshino}}. Kumazawa has been unsuccessful at creating any political change other than some sympathy.
==Analysis== Like most Japanese historical epics, the ''Taiheiki''{{'}}s tendencies towards drama and exaggeration are acknowledged, but the text is regarded as remaining mostly accurate. It is the primary and first-hand source on many of the warriors and battles of this period, and also documents elements of the fall of the powerful and historically important {{ill|Hōjō clan|en|Hōjō clan|WD=Q834364|lt=Hōjō clan}}.<ref name=Sato>{{Cite book |last=Sato |author-link=Hiroaki Sato (translator) |first=Hiroaki |title=Legends of the Samurai |publisher=Overlook Duckworth |year=1995 |ISBN=9781590207307 |pages=157–158}}</ref>
==Cultural references== A line said by the pro-imperial {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|WD=Q38142|lt=Samurai}} [[Kusunoki Masashige]] (regarding how "he wished that he would be reborn seven times to fight for the emperor" in an improved way) in the ''Taiheiki'' became well-known among Japanese nationalists and pro-imperialist in the 19th and 20th centuries and the legends about him became an ideal for them to follow.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Conlan |first=Thomas D. |title=Samurai Weapons & Fighting Techniques |publisher=Amber Books Ltd |year=2022 |isbn=978-1-83886-214-5 |editor-last=Spilling |editor-first=Michael |location=London |pages=113}}</ref>
{{ill|NHK|en|NHK|WD=Q212128|lt=NHK}}'s 1991 {{ill|Taiga drama|en|Taiga drama|WD=Q1200935|lt=Taiga drama}} ''{{ill|Taiheiki (TV series)|en|Taiheiki (TV series)|WD=Q10941476|lt=Taiheiki}}'' was noted for its portrayal of {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|en|Ashikaga Takauji|WD=Q297107|lt=Ashikaga Takauji}} as an agent of change against the decadent Hōjō, rather than a national traitor as generally viewed by Japanese historians.
==See also == * {{ill|Japanese Historical Text Initiative|en|Japanese Historical Text Initiative|WD=Q16973361|lt=Japanese Historical Text Initiative}} * ''{{ill|Shin'yō Wakashū|en|Shin'yō Wakashū|WD=Q3006175|lt=Shin'yō Wakashū}}, Nanboku-chō period collection of waka''
==References== {{Reflist}}
==Further reading== *{{cite book | author=Trans. [[Helen Craig McCullough]] | title=Taiheiki a Chronicle of Medieval Japan | publisher=Tuttle Publishing | year=1979 }}
==External links== *[http://j-texts.com/sheet/thkm.html Complete text (in Japanese)] *[http://www.nancho.net/nancho/otheremp.html Japan's Other Emperor] * Manuscript scans at {{ill|Waseda University Library|en|Waseda University Library|WD=Q7971387|lt=Waseda University Library}}: [http://archive.wul.waseda.ac.jp/kosho/ri05/ri05_12563/ 1603], [http://archive.wul.waseda.ac.jp/kosho/ri05/ri05_02754/ 1698]
{{Classical Japanese literature}}

{{ill|NHK|en|NHK|WD=Q212128|lt=NHK|1=NHK}}'s 1991 {{ill|Taiga drama|en|Taiga drama|WD=Q1200935|lt=Taiga drama|1=Taiga drama}} ''{{ill|Taiheiki (TV series)|en|Taiheiki (TV series)|WD=Q10941476|lt=Taiheiki|1=Taiheiki (TV series)}}'' was noted for its portrayal of {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|en|Ashikaga Takauji|WD=Q297107|lt=Ashikaga Takauji|1=Ashikaga Takauji}} as an agent of change against the decadent Hōjō, rather than a national traitor as generally viewed by Japanese historians.
==See also == * {{ill|Japanese Historical Text Initiative|en|Japanese Historical Text Initiative|WD=Q16973361|lt=Japanese Historical Text Initiative|1=Japanese Historical Text Initiative}} * ''{{ill|Shin'yō Wakashū|en|Shin'yō Wakashū|WD=Q3006175|lt=Shin'yō Wakashū|1=Shin'yō Wakashū}}, Nanboku-chō period collection of waka''
==References== {{Reflist}}
==Further reading== *{{cite book | author=Trans. [[Helen Craig McCullough]] | title=Taiheiki a Chronicle of Medieval Japan | publisher=Tuttle Publishing | year=1979 }}
==External links== *[http://j-texts.com/sheet/thkm.html Complete text (in Japanese)] *[http://www.nancho.net/nancho/otheremp.html Japan's Other Emperor] * Manuscript scans at {{ill|Waseda University Library|en|Waseda University Library|WD=Q7971387|lt=Waseda University Library|1=Waseda University Library}}: [http://archive.wul.waseda.ac.jp/kosho/ri05/ri05_12563/ 1603], [http://archive.wul.waseda.ac.jp/kosho/ri05/ri05_02754/ 1698]
